original_hypothesis.title: TwoWay LockedStatus Enum Defined But Functionally Identical to OneWay - Missing Reverse Functionality
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The IMerge interface defines three LockedStatus values: Locked, OneWay, and TwoWay. However, the onTokenTransfer function only checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`, treating OneWay and TwoWay identically. No code path exists that differentiates TwoWay from OneWay. If TwoWay was intended to enable reversible deposits (returning TGT to depositors in exchange for TITN), this functionality is completely missing. Users or governance processes expecting a TwoWay mode to provide deposit reversal have no recourse.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The enum explicitly names a TwoWay state suggesting bidirectional exchange was planned. The absence of any differentiating code means this is either dead code or missing functionality.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer only checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`; no code anywhere differentiates TwoWay from OneWay; no reverse exchange function exists

preconditions: Users expect TwoWay to enable deposit reversal

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TwoWay enum value exists but has zero functional differentiation from OneWay
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner can set TwoWay but it has no unique effect

operational_questions: Was TwoWay intended to enable reverse exchanges (TITN → TGT)?
Are users or documentation referencing TwoWay functionality?
Is there a planned upgrade to implement TwoWay?
validation_requirements: Confirm no code path differentiates TwoWay from OneWay
Search for any reverse exchange function in the contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users deposit TGT expecting TwoWay mode to allow reversal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: No reversal function exists - TGT is permanently locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users are misled by the TwoWay status into irrevocable deposits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Only owner withdraw() can return TGT

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users may irreversibly deposit TGT under false TwoWay expectations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Only owner can return TGT via withdraw()

validation_result.impact_summary: The `TwoWay` locked status is defined and selectable but lacks any distinct implementation, misleading users who might expect deposit reversibility.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `IMerge` interface defines an enum `LockedStatus` with three values: `Locked`, `OneWay`, and `TwoWay`. The `MergeTgt` contract allows the owner to set the state to any of these values via `setLockedStatus`. However, the only place this state is checked is in `onTokenTransfer`, where it verifies `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`. If the state is `TwoWay`, it bypasses this check and behaves exactly like `OneWay`, allowing deposits. There is no code in the contract that implements 'two-way' functionality (i.e., allowing users to return TITN and withdraw their deposited TGT). This means `TwoWay` is either dead code or an incomplete feature. If the contract is set to `TwoWay`, users might be misled into believing their deposits are reversible, but they will be permanently locked.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence clearly shows that `LockedStatus.TwoWay` is defined and can be set, but is never explicitly handled or differentiated from `OneWay`. No reverse exchange function exists in the contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function only checks if the status is `Locked`. Both `OneWay` and `TwoWay` bypass this check and allow deposits, with no other logic differentiating the two states.
The enum defines `TwoWay`, implying a bidirectional state, but the implementing contract lacks any logic to support it.

calling_sequence: User attempts to find a function to reverse the deposit, but none exists
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The owner sets `lockedStatus` to `TwoWay`. When a user deposits TGT, `onTokenTransfer` checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`, which evaluates to false. The deposit succeeds. The user's TGT is locked in the contract, and there is no function available for the user to reverse this action, making `TwoWay` functionally identical to `OneWay`.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script `arbitrumSetup.ts` sets the status to 1 (`OneWay`), which aligns with the current one-way implementation. However, the owner can change it to `TwoWay` at any time.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: While not a direct exploit for profit, the presence of a `TwoWay` state without corresponding functionality is a logic defect. It can lead to operational disruption if governance or users rely on the implied reversibility of the `TwoWay` state, resulting in irreversibly locked TGT.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Users depositing under the assumption that `TwoWay` allows withdrawals will have their funds permanently locked (only claimable as TITN), as only the owner can withdraw TGT via the admin `withdraw` function.
recommended_mitigations: Remove the `TwoWay` enum value if bidirectional functionality is not intended.
Implement the missing reverse exchange functionality if `TwoWay` is an intended feature.
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy MergeTgt. Call `setLockedStatus(2)`. Deposit TGT via `onTokenTransfer`. Observe that the deposit succeeds but there is no function to reverse it.
uncertainties: It is unclear if `TwoWay` was intended for a future upgrade (though the contract is not upgradeable) or if it is simply a leftover from an earlier design.
additional_testing_needed: Review product requirements to determine if reverse functionality was a planned feature that was accidentally omitted.
